records.ety: [AS. minsian to grow less, dwindle, fr. min small; akin to G. minder less, Goth. minniza less, mins less, adv., L. minor, adj. (cf. Minor); or more likely fr. F. mincer to mince, prob. from (assumed) LL. minutiare. √101. See Minish.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Minced (mnst); p. pr. & vb. n. Minging (mn"sng).]

defs: To cut into very small pieces; to chop fine; to hash; as, to mince meat.

To suppress or weaken the force of; to extenuate; to palliate; to tell by degrees, instead of directly and frankly; to clip, as words or expressions; to utter half and keep back half of; as, he doesn't mince words.
quotes: I know no ways to mince it in love, but directly to say -- “I love you.”
Siren, now mince the sin, And mollify damnation with a phrase.
If, to mince his meaning, I had either omitted some part of what he said, or taken from the strength of his expression, I certainly had wronged him.

defs: To affect; to make a parade of.
To walk with short steps; to walk in a prim, affected manner.
quotes: The daughters of Zion are haughty, and walk with stretched forth necks and wanton eyes, . . . mincing as they go.
I 'll . . . turn two mincing steps Into a manly stride.

defs: To act or talk with affected nicety; to affect delicacy in manner.
A short, precise step; an affected manner.
